source: Sed, Awk & Grep - Regular expressions and wildcards: The Three Swordsmen, and wildcards versus regular expressions

questions.q: Which philosophy does the book open with about GNU/Linux?
questions.why: The 'everything is a file' philosophy is why admins constantly process files, names and content.

questions.q: Who are the 'Three Swordsmen' for processing file content?
questions.why: grep, sed and awk are the potent, frequently-used content-processing trio.

questions.q: What is the key difference between regular expressions and wildcards?
options: Regex match file CONTENT (grep/sed/awk); wildcards match file/dir NAMES (cp/find/mv/touch/ls)
Regex match names; wildcards match content
questions.why: Same symbols, different jobs: regex on content, wildcards (globbing) on names.

questions.q: As a wildcard, what does ? match?
options: Exactly one character of a file/dir name
questions.why: ? matches a single character; * matches zero or more.

questions.q: As a wildcard, what does * match?
options: Zero or more arbitrary characters of a name
questions.why: * matches 0+ characters in a file/dir name.

questions.q: What does the brace expansion {1..3} produce (as in mkdir dir{1..3})?
questions.why: {..} expands a continuous range; {,} (e.g. {1,5,9}) expands a discrete list.

questions.q: What does [0-9] match (as a wildcard or in a bracket expression)?
options: Any single character in the range 0 to 9
questions.why: [-] matches one character within the given range.
